This isn't what we stand for: | Friend --

I'm so disappointed by the way the Republican candidates are talking in this primary. It's not okay to demonize immigrant families and Muslim Americans. It's not okay to call Mexican-Americans "criminals." This country belongs to everyone, not just people who look, worship, or believe any single way.

I think we could all stand to have a little more love and kindness in our lives -- and in this campaign, for that matter. I'm going to keep fighting to tear down the barriers that hold Americans back, from economic inequality to discrimination. I need you fighting by my side.

name: Alex Burgos, MarcoRubio.com
subject: you gotta read this
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Marco Rubio for President
body:
Peter, our campaign staff gets news alerts constantly about all
the stories mentioning Marco. But some of them really stand out,
and this one below did.

It captures so well why Marco is ready to be the underdog. Those
of us who saw him pull off an even more improbable win in 2009
have just total confidence he's going to be able to take down
Donald Trump, win the nomination, and beat Hillary.

He knows adversity, and he's ready for this fight. Give it a read
below, and please share it with friends who are on the fence this
election.

Alex Burgos
Senior Advisor
Marco Rubio for President

---------- Forwarded message ----------
From: Alerts
Date: Fri, Mar 4, 2016 at 8:15 AM
Subject: Palm Beach Post: Shaped by immigrant experience, Rubio
has been underdog and won before
To: alerts@marcorubio.com

Shaped by immigrant experience, Rubio has been underdog and won
before
By George Bennett
Palm Beach Post
March 4, 2016
http://email.vervemail.com/ct/46489617:6aaMdereN:m:3:1349778030:87D2B3A8ED412A505BEDEEF71799AD86:r

Marco Rubio once had a promising future, but now things looked
bleak.

It was April 15, 2009 - tax day, and the day that angry activists
across the U.S. had scheduled the first wave of "tea party"
rallies to protest government bailouts and the agenda of popular
new President Barack Obama. Rubio was booked to speak at a rally
in West Palm Beach as he pondered a long shot U.S. Senate
campaign against popular Florida Gov. Charlie Crist.

Kimberly Mitchell, then a West Palm Beach city commissioner,
recalls driving Rubio to the event and advising him to reconsider
the Senate race.

"I didn't think his chances were all that great. I was driving
him saying, 'Why don't you run for governor?'" Mitchell recalled.
She recounted the story while attending Rubio's 2010 Senate
victory celebration.

Rubio again faces long odds as he pursues the Republican
presidential nomination.

"The pundits say we're underdogs," Rubio declared to a hometown
audience in Miami on Tuesday night as he lost a string of
primaries to Donald Trump. "I'll accept that. We've all been
underdogs. This is a community of underdogs. This is a state of
underdogs. This is a country of underdogs. But we will win."

Rubio and his campaign invoke his 2010 Senate run as evidence
that Rubio's brand of aspirational conservativism and his ability
to communicate - in two languages - with a new generation of
voters can overcome big obstacles and win.

At the opening of a Rubio campaign office in Boca Raton on
Monday, former state House Majority Leader Adam Hasner recalled
the 2010 race as he gave a pep talk to campaign volunteers.

"I was one of the very few people - and I see some of you in this
room today who were in that very small group - when Marco was at
3 percent in the polls, who believed that he could win even in
spite of the fact that people were saying he shouldn't run, that
there was no way he could beat an incumbent governor who was so
popular at the time," said Hasner, a co-chairman of Rubio's
Florida campaign.

"And I think back to 2010 and I remember that in 2010, Marco
Rubio exposed Charlie Crist as the emperor with no clothes. Well
in 2016, Marco Rubio is exposing another emperor, but this time
his clothes are made in China."

But the dynamics this time are more daunting, as Rubio tries to
overtake not only Trump but Texas U.S. Sen. Ted Cruz.

Rubio in 2009 and 2010 rode a wave of anti-establishment
sentiment to the Republican nomination and general election
victory. Now he's part of the political class, trying to coalesce
party regulars and defy an anti-establishment tide.

The Rubio of 2010 appealed to the core conservatism of Republican
primary voters against the more moderate Crist, eventually
driving Crist out of the GOP. But the 2016 Republican
presidential contest hasn't been a traditional race to the right.
Trump, who has reversed himself on a variety issues over the
years, has assembled a coalition of voters who don't seem
particularly concerned with ideological purity.

And to the extent that conservatives still influence Republican
primaries, Rubio faces some skepticism because of his support for
a 2013 immigration reform bill that is loathed by many on the
right.

The immigrant experience has defined Rubio's life and political
career.

As he frequently recounts in his stump speech, Rubio's parents
were born in Cuba and came to the U.S. in the 1950s in search of
a better life. Rubio, born in Miami in 1971, says his parents and
Miami's large Cuban-American community instilled in him an
appreciation of America as a land of opportunity.

Rubio got a law degree from the University of Miami in 1996 and
worked on Bob Dole's losing Republican presidential campaign that
year, running an office in Miami's Little Havana. That introduced
Rubio to the city's byzantine politics and veteran Republican
player Al Cardenas, whose law firm hired Rubio as a land use and
zoning attorney.

Rubio was elected to the West Miami City Commission in 1998 and
to the Florida House in a special election in 2000. He became the
first Cuban-American speaker of the Florida House in 2006. He
helped enact insurance and property tax reforms and an overhaul
of Florida's voting equipment, but didn't discuss his legislative
record when he gave his farewell speech in May 2008.

Instead, Rubio's voice quavered and he frequently paused to gulp
water as he described his parents putting aside their own dreams
so he could pursue his - a phenomenon he said was common for that
generation of Cuban-Americans.

"When I say this about my parents, I say this about my district,
about my community," Rubio said.

Standing on the House floor, where state legislators usually give
speeches about school funding formulas and water management
projects, Rubio delivered an ode to American exceptionalism that
would become a staple of his Senate campaign speeches in 2010 and
his presidential campaign speeches now.

"Do not ever forget the greatness of America," Rubio told his
colleagues near the end of his 2008 farewell address. "Our
greatness doesn't come from our army nor from the size of our
borders but from the compassion of our people and a commitment to
self-government and the rights of all men. And that I hope will
never change.

"Ninety miles from the shores of our very state are men and women
of my ancestry and heritage who still do not have that but they
look to this country for the hope that they one day will. We
cannot fail them because if we do we will fail the world."

Rubio often mentioned his experiences as the son of immigrants as
a 2010 U.S. Senate candidate, but he emphasized that he supported
legal immigration and opposed granting "amnesty" for people in
the country illegally. He praised Ronald Reagan, as Republican
regularly candidates do, but faulted Reagan for signing a 1986
amnesty bill.

During a CNN debate a few weeks before the 2010 election, Crist -
running as a no-party candidate - said he supported an "earned
path to citizenship" rather than amnesty.

"First of all, earned path to citizenship is basically code for
amnesty," Rubio countered.

So some of Rubio's supporters felt betrayed in 2013 when Rubio
became part of a bipartisan "Gang of Eight" pushing an
immigration bill that created a pathway to citizenship for
millions of undocumented people. Rubio argued the bill did not
offer amnesty because it imposed conditions - such as paying
fines, waiting in line and learning English - before granting
citizenship.

The bill passed the Democrat-controlled Senate with opposition
from most Republicans, including eventual 2016 presidential
candidates Cruz and Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky. The
Republican-controlled House never brought the bill up for a vote.

Rubio has since declared that he favors a piecemeal approach to
immigration reform, with proven border security measures in place
before any other provisions are considered.

It's a tougher sell than the "beautiful wall" promised by Trump.
Trump not only leads national polls and delegate counts, but held
a 16-point lead over Rubio in Florida in a recent Quinnipiac
University poll. For Rubio to win the Republican nomination,
he'll first have to come from behind in his home state - just as
he did in 2010.
STOP TRUMP
Paid for by Marco Rubio for President

name: Ben Carson
subject: Peter, thank you.
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Carson America, Inc
body:
---
| | [](http://email.bencarson.com/ct/33978589:s-zXTvCqN:m:1:876315805:1ED1D75C88E8E3AA9ED4FD81407C86DD:r)
---

Peter,

Late yesterday, I announced that I am suspending my campaign for President. I'd like to thank you for your incredible support and tell you more about my decision, my thoughts on the campaign overall, and my plans moving forward.

I have lived a blessed life and achieved more success than I ever dreamed was possible.

Running for President was never on my bucket list, and when I was drafted by a grassroots movement asking me to do so, it was not a decision that I made lightly.

However, I believed that my values, life experience and common sense solutions put me in a position to help get our nation back on track for the sake of our children and grandchildren. This grueling endeavor would not have been worth it for any other reason.

When I began exploring a run for President as a private citizen detached from the political class, I had neither a political team nor a national network of wealthy donors standing by waiting to support me.

We had to build a grassroots campaign from scratch by reaching out to as many people as possible, as quickly as possible and through every means possible, resulting in indisputable success.

This movement consistently outraised the entire Republican field, got onto every ballot and built a 50-state, bricks-and-mortar campaign infrastructure to share my story, values and solutions.

It was an historic feat, and I'll be forever grateful to my team and my incredible supporters, financial and otherwise, from all corners of America.

I hope my presence added a measure of civility to the race, raised issues that would not otherwise have been discussed and had an overall positive impact. While our political efforts must come to a close, gratefully, this grassroots movement has given new voice to "We the People" and inspired millions.

I may be departing the campaign trail, but I will not be departing the scene. Instead, for the rest of my life, I will continue to work tirelessly to do everything I can to save America for the next generation.

I will be working on a number of initiatives, including serving as honorary chair of My Faith Votes, a non-profit organization dedicated to mobilizing the 25 million evangelicals who didn't vote in the last election. The twin pillars of faith and family are under attack, and I will work to strengthen our nation by preserving them both.

I have committed to not endorse a specific individual, but rather "We the People." Though many today are making decisions based on fear and anger, I trust their judgment to logically examine the candidates and make the right decision by looking at:

• whether they've demonstrated significant accomplishments over their lives and careers;
• if they have ideas that are clear and policies that are easy to find;
• how they treat their family and others, as that is how they will lead the country;
• what they have done to improve the lives of their fellow Americans;
• the people they are with, what they are saying and how they collaborate with others;
• their ethics, because what America needs is "trickle-down ethics."

Conservatives should not be embarrassed by capitalism but must couple it with compassion to lift people out of a culture of dependency and provide ladders of opportunity for all Americans to be a part of the fabric of society.

People need to understand this is a most important election, in which we are deciding whether we allow the government to dictate our rights and take care of our needs, or whether individuals will rise up and take responsibility in an atmosphere of opportunity for all.

The bottom line is, "We the People" are the ones making the decisions; but in order to do that, we must become active and informed, not manipulated by the political class and media.

Along with millions of patriots who have supported my campaign for President, I remain committed to saving America for future generations. We must not depart from our goal of restoring what God and our Founders intended for this exceptional nation.

Equally important, we need to understand that Republicans are not each others' enemies. We need to engage in conversation and challenge each others' positions, not fight each other. Conservatives need to unify so that that we do not snatch defeat from the jaws of victory and put another secular progressive in the White House.

Again, thank you for your support. I will never forget you, and I hope we'll be able to work side by side for years to come.

Sincerely,

Ben Carson

**

**
Carson America, Inc | P.O. Box 26261 | Alexandria, VA 22313**

clean_body: | | Peter, Late yesterday, I announced that I am suspending my campaign for President. I'd like to thank you for your incredible support and tell you more about my decision, my thoughts on the campaign overall, and my plans moving forward. I have lived a blessed life and achieved more success than I ever dreamed was possible. Running for President was never on my bucket list, and when I was drafted by a grassroots movement asking me to do so, it was not a decision that I made lightly. However, I believed that my values, life experience and common sense solutions put me in a position to help get our nation back on track for the sake of our children and grandchildren. This grueling endeavor would not have been worth it for any other reason. When I began exploring a run for President as a private citizen detached from the political class, I had neither a political team nor a national network of wealthy donors standing by waiting to support me. We had to build a grassroots campaign from scratch by reaching out to as many people as possible, as quickly as possible and through every means possible, resulting in indisputable success. This movement consistently outraised the entire Republican field, got onto every ballot and built a 50-state, bricks-and-mortar campaign infrastructure to share my story, values and solutions. It was an historic feat, and I'll be forever grateful to my team and my incredible supporters, financial and otherwise, from all corners of America. I hope my presence added a measure of civility to the race, raised issues that would not otherwise have been discussed and had an overall positive impact. While our political efforts must come to a close, gratefully, this grassroots movement has given new voice to "We the People" and inspired millions. I may be departing the campaign trail, but I will not be departing the scene. Instead, for the rest of my life, I will continue to work tirelessly to do everything I can to save America for the next generation. I will be working on a number of initiatives, including serving as honorary chair of My Faith Votes, a non-profit organization dedicated to mobilizing the 25 million evangelicals who didn't vote in the last election. The twin pillars of faith and family are under attack, and I will work to strengthen our nation by preserving them both. I have committed to not endorse a specific individual, but rather "We the People." Though many today are making decisions based on fear and anger, I trust their judgment to logically examine the candidates and make the right decision by looking at: • whether they've demonstrated significant accomplishments over their lives and careers; • if they have ideas that are clear and policies that are easy to find; • how they treat their family and others, as that is how they will lead the country; • what they have done to improve the lives of their fellow Americans; • the people they are with, what they are saying and how they collaborate with others; • their ethics, because what America needs is "trickle-down ethics." Conservatives should not be embarrassed by capitalism but must couple it with compassion to lift people out of a culture of dependency and provide ladders of opportunity for all Americans to be a part of the fabric of society. People need to understand this is a most important election, in which we are deciding whether we allow the government to dictate our rights and take care of our needs, or whether individuals will rise up and take responsibility in an atmosphere of opportunity for all. The bottom line is, "We the People" are the ones making the decisions; but in order to do that, we must become active and informed, not manipulated by the political class and media. Along with millions of patriots who have supported my campaign for President, I remain committed to saving America for future generations. We must not depart from our goal of restoring what God and our Founders intended for this exceptional nation. Equally important, we need to understand that Republicans are not each others' enemies. We need to engage in conversation and challenge each others' positions, not fight each other. Conservatives need to unify so that that we do not snatch defeat from the jaws of victory and put another secular progressive in the White House. Again, thank you for your support. I will never forget you, and I hope we'll be able to work side by side for years to come. Sincerely, Ben Carson Carson America, Inc | P.O. body:
www.JoeSestak.com
CCUP OF JOE

March 5th, 2015
Newsletter

Good morning! I wanted to provide an update about what I've been up to and where I've traveled. Thanks so much for reading, I truly appreciate your support!

Sestak Meets with Supporters

Written by Ryan Brown
The Altoona Mirror

Strategic thinking played a major role in Senate hopeful Joe Sestak's address to Democrats Wednesday in Altoona.

But Sestak rejected the suggestion, widely detailed in the national media, that Pennsylvania's Senate race should be a skirmish in a strategic battle between Democrats and Republicans. As the national parties raise millions in a battle for a Senate majority, Sestak - one of three Democrats vying to unseat Sen. Pat Toomey, R-Pa. - said he's keeping his focus local.

"They look at it as 'We want Pennsylvania, so you all just follow our wisdom,'" Sestak, a former congressman and Navy admiral, said of some in the national party establishment.

Instead, he's carried out an intensely local campaign, having visited seven cities in a 36-hour period this week. Of four candidates including Toomey, Sestak remains the only one to have stopped by Altoona this election cycle.

Sestak met with a small group of Democrats and supporters Wednesday afternoon at Lena's Cafe, addressing national security and the need for strong diplomacy. He criticized congressional Republicans who, in his view, have impeded diplomatic efforts and rejected the need for nonmilitary solutions overseas.

"'Cut the State Department because they're a bunch of pansy-wansy suits,'" he said, mocking the attitude of some in Washington. "(But) diplomacy matters. It matters hugely."

While Sestak focused on military matters and entanglements abroad, many political observers have pointed to domestic affairs as the deciding factors in this November's election season. With 34 Senate seats up for grabs and Democrats hoping to retake a majority, the ongoing fight over a Supreme Court nominee could prove a decisive issue.

Toomey has joined fellow Republicans in pre-emptively rejecting any vote for President Barack Obama's nominee, no matter whom he chooses to replace the late Justice Antonin Scalia. Last week, Sestak called on Toomey to "put his duty ... ahead of obstructionism."

Sestak is vying with two fellow Democrats in the April primary: Braddock Mayor John Fetterman and former state official Katie McGinty, who has the backing of much of the Democratic establishment.

Whoever wins the primary will be set for a hard fight against Toomey, whose seat could be targeted by national Democratic groups.

Addressing the small group of supporters and fellow Democrats, Sestak reflected fondly on his past fights against the Democratic establishment - including his 2010 primary battle with Arlen Specter, who had the backing of the national party machine. He said his strategy at that time, meeting frequently with voters rather than spending money, would serve him again in 2016.

"I think that's the best strategy," he said. "It isn't about party leadership. It's about people."

Stopping By State Committee
to Talk Policy

It was great stopping by State Committee in Hershey to see friends from all across the state, and talk about the issues important to our Democratic Party!

Yesterday I stopped by the Seniors, Veterans, and Rural caucuses to talk policy and then spoke at a forum.

SPECIAL SECTION:
PRE-PRIMARY POLITICS

I usually reserve the Cup of Joe to tell you where I've been, who I've
met, and what we discussed. But as we get closer to the primary
with just 7 weeks to go, I thought I would give you a rundown of
the current political situation by highlighting the below articles,
which you can click to read:

The Reading Eagle
The Rundown: Joe Sestak Leads U.S. Senate Race
http://www.readingeagle.com/news/article/the-rundown-joe-sestak-leads-democratic-us-senate-race

The Washington Post
The top 10 Senate races of 2016
https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2016/03/04/the-top-10-senate-races-in-2016/
5. Pennsylvania (R): Perhaps nowhere is the impact of Trump's potential nomination as clear as in the swing state of Pennsylvania. If Trump is the nominee, the state goes from lean Republican to a potential Democratic pickup, according to Sabato and Kondik. But supporters of Sen. Patrick J. Toomey (R) are comforted by the fact he can continue to raise money while Democrats muddle through a competitive April primary. Former congressman Joe Sestak is trying to avenge his hairpin loss to Toomey in 2010, but most of the Democratic brass have put their money behind Katie McGinty, the former chief of staff to Pennsylvania Gov. Tom Wolf (D). (Although Jonathan Tamari of the Philadelphia Inquirer recently reported some of the state's Democrats are underwhelmed with McGinty's campaign so far.) Still, like many of the states that top our list, the fact that Pennsylvania went for Obama twice will make it difficult for Republicans to hang onto it in a presidential year, no matter how perfect of a campaign their incumbents run.

The Philadelphia Inquirer
As primary nears, Dems wonder: Can McGinty rally?
http://www.philly.com/philly/news/politics/20160304_As_primary_nears__Dems_wonder__Can_McGinty_rally_.html#DldXhPcWwGvT5Wsz.99
WASHINGTON - For a race that they hope could help reshape the Senate, Democratic insiders in Washington and Pennsylvania last year tapped Katie McGinty, an energetic, well-connected, and loyal party player as their choice to unseat Republican Sen. Pat Toomey.
But with just seven weeks to go until the April 26 primary, and the party faithful gathering this weekend in Hershey, many Pennsylvania Democrats are still waiting for her campaign to gain traction.
In interviews this week, more than a dozen Pennsylvania Democratic operatives and officials - most of them uncommitted in the race - largely praised McGinty's resumé and intelligence.
But they also flagged warning signs. A majority said her campaign had so far left them, and party activists, flat. Her message, they said, has been too generic, tethered to well-worn talking points such as raising the minimum wage and "fighting for the middle class."
They questioned the return on her spending: McGinty burned through $7 of every $10 raised last quarter without any gains in polling. Her campaign is now counting on outside support for a TV push over the finish line.

Thanks for reading -- and please click the "contribute" button to keep our momentum going.

Joe

We have the momentum, but the fight against Donald Trump and his billions is nowhere close to over -- Setting this fundraising record will be another huge win for Ted's campaign when we need it most. Thanks for all that you do, and please keep supporting my husband in this grueling campaign! Best, Heidi Cruz P.S. Did I mention we're about to set a record? body:

I'll be direct, sisters and brothers: we're in an intense stretch of the campaign where **success for Bernie means the political and media establishment must take note.**
Kansas, Louisiana, and Nebraska all vote or caucus today. Maine caucuses tomorrow, and then at 8 p.m. Sunday Bernie takes the stage for the next Democratic debate in Michigan, where voters go to the polls on Tuesday.
I think we will do very well in the next couple days. It's going to be intense, and I know Bernie would love to know that you're with him.
[**Make a $2.70 contribution to our campaign to send a message to the establishment that Bernie's on the path to winning the Democratic nomination and the White House.**](https://secure.actblue.com/contribute/page/three-states?refcode=em160305pm1-full-e "https://secure.actblue.com/contribute/page/three-states?refcode=em160305pm1-full-e")

Jeff Weaver
